--- 
title: "The Loveliest R Tip of the Day"
subtitle: "aRtsy and highcharter package"
author: "Mvuselelo Mathunjwa"
date: "7, May, 2026"
format:
  revealjs: 
    slide-number: true
    chalkboard: 
      buttons: true
    preview-links: auto
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
theme: serif
title-slide-attributes:
  data-background-color: "#FFD1DC"
  data-color: "#FF1493"
---

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(gapminder)
library(aRtsy)
library(highcharter)
library(gapminder)
```


## What is aRtsy? {background="#FFD1DC" .smaller transition="slide"}

aRtsy is an r pacakge used to create generative art using mathematics, algorithms, randomness, and data-based patterns. This package can be used to:

::: incremental
-   Create abstract, mathematical art
-   Visualise data creatively
-   Experiment with algorithms and symmetry 
-   Combine programming with design

 ![](Artwork.png){.absolute .fragment top="360" left="10" width="350" height="400"}

 ![](Artwork2.png){.absolute .fragment top="360" left="360" width="360" height="400"}

 ![](myArtwork.png){.absolute .fragment top="360" left="700" width="350" height="400"}
:::

. . .

## aRtsy Example {auto-animate="true" background="#FFD1DC" .smaller transition="slide"}


```{r}
#| echo: true
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 4.5
library(aRtsy)

canvas_flow(
  colors = colorPalette("blossom"),
   background = "#fafafa",
  lines = 900,
  lwd = 0.05,
  iterations = 100,
  stepmax = 0.01,
  outline = c("none", "circle", "square"),
  polar = FALSE,
  angles = NULL
)
```

## Why is aRtsy useful? {background="#FFD1DC" .smaller transition="slide"}

::: incremental
-   SUPRISE!! IT IS NOT, ATLEAST NOT YET.
-   The most we can do with it at this point is use it as a comparison to traditional plots.
-   I would say most of it's usefulness comes from firing up data scientists' artistic spirit. 
-   The night is still young though. There is still more exploration to be done with this package!

![](Dance_Fox_Gif.gif){.absolute .fragment top="370" left="250" width="600" height="400"}
:::

## What is highcharter? {background="#FFD1DC" .smaller transition="slide"}

::: {.fragment .fade-in}
Highcharter is an R package that serves as a wrapper for the "Highcharts" JavaScript charting library. It creates interactive graphs and animated visualisations.
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-in}
Highcharter also supports many different graph types like:
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-in}
- Line graphs
- Scatterplots
- Bar charts
- Pie charts 
- Heat maps
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-in}
Unlike normal graphs, highcharter has interactive elements that allow you to:
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-in}
- Hover over points
- Zoom into graphs
- Hide or show categories
:::

## Example 1: Function Explanation {background="#FFD1DC" .smaller transition="slide"}

-   It is very similar to ggplot and easy to use

``` {.python code-line-numbers="9-10|12-17|19-24"}
library(highcharter)
library(gapminder)
library(dplyr)

big4 <- gapminder |>
  filter(country %in% c("Niger","Swaziland", "Tanzania", "South Africa")) |>
  arrange(year)

# Creates empty highcharters object  
highchart() |>

# Inputs variables needed for graph 
  hc_add_series(data = big4,
                type = "line", 
                hcaes(x = year,
                      y = pop, 
                      group = country)) |>
  
  # Sets labels for axis
  hc_xAxis(title = list(text="Year")) |>
  hc_yAxis(title = list(text="Population")) |>
  hc_plotOptions(series = list(marker = list(symbol = "circle"))) |>
  hc_legend(align = "right", 
            verticalAlign = "top") 
```

## Example 1: Interactive Line Graph {background="#FFD1DC" .smaller transition="slide"}

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 7
library(gapminder)
library(dplyr)

big4 <- gapminder |>
  filter(country %in% c("Niger","Swaziland", "Tanzania", "South Africa")) |>
  arrange(year)

highchart() |>
  hc_add_series(data = big4,
                type = "line", 
                hcaes(x = year,
                      y = pop, 
                      group = country)) |>
  hc_xAxis(title = list(text="Year")) |>
  hc_yAxis(title = list(text="Population")) |>
  hc_plotOptions(series = list(marker = list(symbol = "circle")), animation = TRUE) |>
  hc_legend(align = "right", 
            verticalAlign = "top") 
```

## Example 2: Interactive Pie Graph {background="#FFD1DC" .smaller transition="slide"}

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 7

big4_2 <- big4 |>
  select(country, pop, year) |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarise(total_pop = sum(pop))

highchart() |>
  hc_add_series(data = big4_2,
                type = "pie", 
                hcaes(x = country,
                      y = total_pop)) |>
  hc_title(text = "Population by Country") |>
      hc_subtitle(text = "Four African countries by population")
```

## Usefulness and Limitations {background="#FFD1DC" .smaller transition="slide"}

| Pros                                 | Cons                                         |
|--------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| Dashboard and Shiny friendly         | Graphs rely on HTML and will not show up with rendered PDFS.|
| Good for visualisation    | More focused on visualisation than analysis        |
| Interactive and looks good    | Relies on JavaScript backend                                           |
| Flexible since there is acess to many different types of graphs  |  Can get cluttered with big datasets  |

# THANK YOU FOR LISTENING :3 {background="#FFD1DC" .smaller transition="slide"}
